ideas.title: Seasonality as a Non-Linear Dynamical System: Adapting Climate Tipping Point Analysis
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that changes in seasonality (e.g., earlier springs, longer summers) are simple, linear responses to global warming. By analogy with the study of climate tipping points, it proposes to investigate seasonality as a non-linear, chaotic system with critical feedback loops. We adapt the solution from climate science—using dynamical systems theory, bifurcation analysis, and the search for early-warning signals—to this context. The adapted idea is to model seasonal cycles as potential regimes in a dynamical system, identify reinforcing feedbacks (e.g., snow-albedo, evapotranspiration-precipitation), and analyze high-resolution historical climate data for statistical precursors (like increased autocorrelation or variance) that signal an impending shift in seasonal patterns, moving the field from trend projection to the analysis of stability and abrupt change.

ideas.title: Decompose and Attribute Complex Temporal Variation in Seasonality Using Methods from Population Genetics
ideas.description: This research addresses the underlying drivers for inconsistent and complex changes in seasonality patterns over long periods by drawing an analogy from population genetics. In that field, complex temporal variation in genetic traits is solved by decomposing total variation into components like directional selection, fluctuating selection, genetic drift, and gene flow. Adapting this solution, we propose to treat metrics of seasonality as climate system traits and develop a statistical attribution framework. This framework will separate the observed long-term variation into contributions from: 1) secular anthropogenic forcing (a directional driver), 2) low-frequency internal climate variability (fluctuating drivers like ocean oscillations), 3) high-frequency stochastic atmospheric noise, and 4) teleconnected influences from distant systems. The outcome will be a quantified understanding of which drivers are responsible for observed complexities and inconsistencies in seasonal shifts across different regions and time periods.

ideas.title: Apply Geophysical Signal Separation to Visitation Data
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from geophysics, where techniques like Empirical Mode Decomposition are used to separate composite seismic signals into intrinsic mode functions representing different physical processes (e.g., body waves from deep earthquakes, surface waves, and local noise). Adapting this solution, we treat temporal visitation data as a geophysical signal. The goal is to apply adaptive, data-driven decomposition methods (such as EMD or its variants) to intrinsically separate the visitation signal into components representing a long-term trend (analogous to tectonic shifts), complex seasonal or cyclical patterns (analogous to surface waves with potentially multiple, non-stationary periods), and irregular residuals (analogous to noise or transient events). This approach is particularly valuable for visitation data with non-stationary or evolving seasonal patterns, as it does not require pre-defining seasonal periods like traditional financial decomposition methods, offering a more flexible analysis of underlying visitation drivers.

ideas.title: A Detection and Attribution Framework for Chaotic Signatures in Climate Seasonality
ideas.description: This research moves beyond theoretical critique to develop a rigorous, testable methodology for identifying chaotic dynamics in seasonal changes. It constructs a framework that pits linear, non-linear deterministic (chaotic), and stochastic models against each other using both simulated and observed climate data. The approach applies advanced diagnostics from dynamical systems theory to high-resolution proxy records and instrumental data to search for unambiguous signatures of low-dimensional chaos. The final output is a set of quantifiable, attributable criteria to determine where and when seasonality behaves as a chaotic system versus a forced linear trend or a noisy process.
